# Australia — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. MIT, Apache-2.0, GPL-3.0, and AGPL-3.0 content can all be incorporated with attribution. Projects below are license-compatible unless noted otherwise.

## PolicyEngine Australia

- Repository: [PolicyEngine/policyengine-au](https://github.com/PolicyEngine/policyengine-au)
- License: AGPL-3.0
- Language: English
- Scope: Full Australian tax-benefit microsimulation model covering personal income tax, Medicare levy, HECS-HELP repayment thresholds, and superannuation rules.
- Why it matters: Comprehensive, actively maintained microsimulation with detailed modelling of Australian tax and transfer policy. Strong validation source for PIT brackets, offsets, and levy calculations.
- Integration approach:
  - AGPL-3.0 is the same license family as OpenAccountants.

# Belgium Capital Gains — v1.0

## Core rule: generally no CGT for private investors

Belgium does **not** have a general capital gains tax for private investors who
manage their own portfolio in the normal course. Gains from disposal of shares,
bonds, and other securities by a **private individual acting as a normal investor**
are **not taxed**.

---

## When gains ARE taxable

Gains on financial assets become taxable as **miscellaneous income (diverse inkomsten)**
if:
1. The person is engaged in **speculative behaviour** (fr

# Canada — Non-Resident Capital Gains & Section 116 — v1.0

## Core rule

Non-residents of Canada are taxed on capital gains from **Taxable Canadian Property (TCP)**.
Gains from non-TCP assets: **no Canadian tax**.

---

## tesoro

- Repository: [libo/tesoro](https://github.com/libo/tesoro)
- License: MIT
- Stars: 24
- Language: Ruby
- Scope: Danish capital gains tax calculator for stock/investment portfolios. Implements the two-tier aktieindkomst (share income) taxation system with progressive rates: 27% up to progressionsgrænsen (DKK 58,900 in 2023) and 42% above. Supports married/unmarried filing with combined progressive threshold for married couples (2×).
- Why it matters: Clean implementation of SKAT's aktieindkomst progressive system with historical threshold data from 2014–2023. MIT license.

Trigger on phrases like "prepare CA3", "French VAT return", "TVA", "déclaration de TVA", "classify these transactions for French VAT", or any request involving France VAT filing. MUST be loaded alongside BOTH vat-workflow-base v0.1 or later (for workflow architecture) AND eu-vat-directive v0.1 or later (for EU directive content). ALWAYS read this skill before touching any French VAT work.

# France — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. All projects below have compatible licenses.

## OpenFisca France

- Repository: [openfisca/openfisca-france](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france)
- License: AGPL-3.0
- Language: French
- Stars: 296
- Scope: Complete model of the French tax and benefit system — income tax, social contributions, housing benefits, family benefits, and more. Encodes French fiscal law as executable Python code with a web API.
- Why it matters: This is the gold standard for rules-as-code in France. Maintained by a dedicated organisation, used by French government services, and covers far more than just income tax.
- Integration: Same license (AGPL-3.0). Content, formulas, parameter values, and test cases can be directly referenced or adapted with attribution.

## Calculette

description: >
  Use this skill whenever asked about German Trade Tax (Gewerbesteuer / GewSt) for self-employed Gewerbetreibende. Trigger on phrases like "Gewerbesteuer", "trade tax Germany", "GewSt", "Hebesatz", "Gewerbeertrag", "Steuermessbetrag", "Freibetrag 24500", "Gewerbesteuer Anrechnung", "§35 EStG", "trade tax credit", "Hinzurechnungen", "Kürzungen", "GewSt 1 A", or any question about German municipal trade tax obligations. ALWAYS read this skill before touching any Gewerbesteuer work.

# Germany — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. All projects below have compatible licenses.

## LstGen

- Repository: [jenner/LstGen](https://github.com/jenner/LstGen)
- License: MIT
- Stars: 65
- Language: Python (generator), outputs Python/Java/JavaScript/Go/PHP
- Scope: Code generator that produces working payroll tax (Lohnsteuer) calculators from the official BMF Programmablaufplan (PAP) XML files.
- Why it matters: The German Federal Ministry of Finance publishes tax calculation logic as XML flowcharts. LstGen turns those into runnable code — the most direct possible source of truth for German wage tax.
- Data extracted: PAP structure, input/output parameter definitions, usage patterns for all Steuerklassen.
- Integration: MIT. Formulas and logic adapted into `de-payroll.md` with attribution.

# Ghana — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. Projects below are used as reference for tax rate data.

## taxcalculatorgh

- Repository: [Kessir/taxcalculatorgh](https://github.com/Kessir/taxcalculatorgh)
- License: verify before code reuse (no explicit license file)
- Stars: 32
- Language: JavaScript (Vue.js)
- Scope: Most popular Ghana income tax calculator on GitHub. Implements GRA PAYE (Pay As You Earn) monthly tax brackets with historical rate tables from 2021 through 2024. Includes SSNIT (Social Security) contribution calculation at 5.5%.
- Why it matters: Actively maintained with bracket updates tracking GRA announcements. 32 stars indicates real usage among Ghanaian developers and taxpayers.
- Integration approach: Monthly PAYE bracket tables and SSNIT rate used as reference for the income tax skill.

Trigger on phrases like "GST return", "GSTR-1", "GSTR-3B", "GSTR-9", "file GST", "GST classification", "HSN code", "SAC code", "input tax credit", "ITC", "reverse charge", "e-invoicing", "e-way bill", "CGST", "SGST", "IGST", "inter-state supply", "intra-state supply", "place of supply", or any request involving Indian indirect tax compliance. This skill covers regular (non-composition) GST-registered taxpayers with a single state GSTIN only. Composition scheme, multi-state, SEZ, e-commerce operator, and ISD registrations are in the refusal catalogue. ALWAYS read this skill before touching any India GST work.

# India — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. All projects below have compatible licenses.

## taxcalcindia

- Repository: [amrajacivil/taxcalcindia](https://github.com/amrajacivil/taxcalcindia)
- License: MIT
- Language: English
- Scope: Python package for Indian income tax calculation. Supports old and new regimes, salary/business/capital gains income, common deductions (80C, 80D, HRA, professional tax), and slab-wise breakdown.

frontmatter.description: Use this skill whenever asked to prepare, review, or classify transactions for an Iran VAT return. Trigger on phrases like "Iran VAT", "INTA tax", "Iran value added tax". ALWAYS read this skill before handling any Iran VAT work.

Trigger on phrases like "Isle of Man VAT", "IOM VAT", "Manx VAT", "Isle of Man tax return", or any request involving Isle of Man VAT. # Italy — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. All projects below have compatible licenses.

## De-CAF (Modello Redditi PF)

- Repository: [vjt/decaf](https://github.com/vjt/decaf)
- License: MIT
- Language: Italian
- Scope: Generates Modello Redditi PF (quadri RW, RT, RL) from foreign broker data (Interactive Brokers, Schwab). Handles IVAFE, capital gains on securities, forex FIFO, and BCE exchange rates.
- Integration: MIT. Foreign investment reporting logic, IVAFE calculation, and Modello Redditi field mapping directly usable.

## BonusPerMe

- Repository: [nogarasimone/bonusperme](https://github.com/nogarasimone/bonusperme)
- License: AGPL-3.0
- Language: Italian (7 languages)
- Scope: Identifies 40+ national and regional Italian bonuses, tax deductions, and subsidies based on ISEE, income, and family comp

- Repository: [kazukinagata/shinkoku](https://github.com/kazukinagata/shinkoku)
- License: MIT
- Language: Japanese
- Scope: AI-agent plugin for Japanese tax filing automation, including bookkeeping, income tax, consumption tax, settlement workflows, e-Tax guidance, and browser-assisted filing through the National Tax Agency's 確定申告書等作成コーナー.
- Why it matters: This is the strongest known Japan-native open-source project in the same problem space as OpenAccountants. It is built around agent skills, uses official Japanese tax terminology, and covers practical workflows beyond pure tax formulas.
- Integration approach:

# Lithuania — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. All projects below have compatible licenses. Projects below can be incorporated with attribution.

## income-tax-calculator

- Repository: [sarunas/income-tax-calculator](https://github.com/sarunas/income-tax-calculator)
- License: MIT
- Stars: 21
- Language: TypeScript
- Scope: Lithuanian tax tool that converts IBI (investment brokerage) reports to VMI (Lithuanian tax authority) tax declaration format. Handles capital gains and investment income reporting for Lithuanian taxpayers.
- Integration: MIT. Freely usable with attribution. IBI-to-VMI conversion logic and declaration format mappings can be adapted.

## What this file is

**Obligation category:** CT (Consumption Tax)
**Functional role:** Return preparation
**Status:** Complete

This is a Tier 2 content skill for preparing the Mexican IVA monthly return for self-employed individuals (personas fisicas con actividad empresarial or regimen de servicios profesionales). Mexico has a single standard rate (16%) plus a zero rate for essential goods and exports, with monthly filing.

# New Zealand — Capital Gains — v1.0

## Core rule: no general CGT

New Zealand does **not** have a general capital gains tax. Gains from the disposal of
most assets — shares, bonds, investment properties held long-term — are **not taxed**.

However, several regimes effectively tax certain gains:

---

frontmatter.Use this skill whenever asked about Para: in Paraguay the employer is generally NOT an income-tax (IRP) withholding agent on dependent salaries — IRP is self-assessed annually by the individual. The employer's mandatory payroll burden is IPS social security (employee 9% + employer 16.5% commercial). This skill covers IPS contributions, the minimum wage, the mandatory aguinaldo (13th salary), the IRP self-assessment position, monthly IPS filing, and payslip/income-certificate obligations. ALWAYS read this skill before processing any Paraguay payroll.

# Saudi Arabia — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. All projects below have compatible licenses (MIT).

## SallaApp/ZATCA

- Repository: [SallaApp/ZATCA](https://github.com/SallaApp/ZATCA)
- License: MIT
- Language: PHP
- Stars: 160
- Scope: ZATCA (Fatoora) e-invoicing implementation maintained by Salla (Saudi e-commerce platform). Covers Phase 1 QR code generation and Phase 2 invoice signing (CSR generation, XML signing, ZATCA API integration). TLV encoding for the 5 mandatory QR fields (seller name, VAT registration number, timestamp, invoice total, VAT total). Includes certificate handling for ZATCA's FATOORA platform.
- Tax computation data: Confirms VAT is a single-rate system (examples use 15%). TLV Tag 5 = invoice VAT total. Confirms 15-digit TIN format. No standalone rate tables — the VAT rate

Trigger on phrases like "prepare Swiss VAT return", "MWST Abrechnung", "Swiss VAT", "Saldosteuersatz", "Bezugsteuer", or any request involving Swiss VAT filing. Also trigger when classifying transactions for VAT purposes from bank statements, invoices, or other source data. This skill covers Switzerland only and only the effektive Abrechnungsmethode (effective method). Saldosteuersatz (flat-rate), Pauschalsteuersatz, and Gruppenbesteuerung are in the refusal catalogue. Switzerland is NOT in the EU — there are no intra-community acquisitions. MUST be loaded alongside vat-workflow-base v0.1 or later (for workflow architecture). Do NOT load eu-vat-directive — it does not apply to Switzerland. ALWAYS read this skill before touching any Swiss VAT work.

# United Kingdom — Related Open-Source Projects

OpenAccountants is AGPL-3.0. All projects below have compatible licenses.

## HMRC Tax Kalculator

- Repository: [hmrc/tax-kalculator](https://github.com/hmrc/tax-kalculator)
- License: Apache-2.0
- Language: English / Kotlin
- Stars: 16
- Scope: Kotlin Multiplatform take-home pay calculator covering income tax, National Insurance, and student loan repayments. Official HMRC repository.
- Why it matters: This is actual HMRC code — the tax authority's own open-source implementation.
- Integration: Apache-2.0 flows into AGPL-3.0. Logic and calculation patterns can be adapted with attribution.

## SavingTool HMRC Income Tax

- Repository: [SavingTool/hmrc-income-tax](https://github.com/SavingTool/hmrc-income-tax)
- License: MIT
- Language: English / TypeScript
- Stars: 22
- Scope: TypeScrip

frontmatter.name: uruguay-payroll
frontmatter.Use this skill whenever asked about Urug: unlike many Latin-American jurisdictions, in Uruguay the employer IS an IRPF withholding agent on dependent salaries — IRPF (Categoría II) is withheld monthly on a progressive scale and reconciled by a year-end ajuste anual, on top of mandatory BPS contributions. This skill covers IRPF withholding, BPS contributions (jubilatorio, FONASA, FRL, FGCL), the retirement contribution ceiling, the minimum wage, the IRPF annual sworn declaration, and filing/payslip obligations. ALWAYS read this skill before processing any Uruguay payroll.

frontmatter.name: uruguay-social-contributions
frontmatter.Use this skill whenever asked about Urug: BPS social contributions (jubilatorio/montepío, FONASA, FRL, FGCL) are SEPARATE from IRPF. IRPF (Impuesto a la Renta de las Personas Físicas, Categoría II) is a distinct DGI progressive income tax expressed in BPC units — it is NOT a social contribution. This skill computes ONLY the BPS contribution layer; IRPF withholding lives in the uruguay-payroll / uruguay-income-tax skills. This skill covers the jubilatorio/montepío rates, the FONASA health matrix (3%–8% by income and dependants), FRL, FGCL, the retirement contribution ceiling, the minimum wage, classification of BPS-related bank transactions, and the boundary with IRPF. ALWAYS read this skill before computing any Uruguay social contribution.

# US Tax Residency (Non-Citizens) — v1.0

## Core rule

For non-US-citizens, US tax residency (as a "resident alien") is determined by two tests.
Meet **either** test → resident alien (taxed on worldwide income like a US citizen).
Meet **neither** → non-resident alien (taxed only on US-source income).

README.raw:
# Alaska — OpenAccountants Tax Skills

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| State | Alaska |
| Code | US-AK |
| Income tax | No |
| Sales tax | No (local only — no statewide sales tax) |
| Franchise / gross receipts tax | No (for individuals / sole proprietors) |
| Tax authority | [Alaska Department of Revenue](https://www.tax.alaska.gov) |
| Quality tier | Research-verified |

## Key facts

- Alaska has **no individual income tax** (one of nine states with no income tax).
- Alaska has **no statewide sales tax**; however, some local jurisdictions (boroughs and cities) levy local sales taxes ranging from 1% to 7.5%.
- Alaska has **no franchise tax** applicable to sole proprietors.
- Alaska does levy a corporate income tax on corporations (not applicable to sole proprietors/individuals).
- The Alaska Permanent Fund Dividend (PFD) is taxable on the **federal** return but there is no state income tax consequence.

frontmatter.name: ca-estimated-tax-540es
frontmatter.description: Tier 2 California content skill for computing California estimated tax payments under Form 540-ES for full-year California residents who are sole proprietors or single-member LLCs disregarded for federal tax. Covers the California 30/40/0/30 installment schedule (NOT equal quarterly like federal), due dates (April 15, June 15, September 15, January 15), the 100% prior-year safe harbor, underpayment penalty computation on Form 5805 / 5805-F, required annual payment rules, interaction with federal estimated tax (separate payments to separate agencies), and withholding credits from W-2 or backup withholding. Defers income tax computation to ca-540-individual-return and SMLLC franchise tax to ca-smllc-form-568. MUST be loaded alongside us-tax-workflow-base v0.1 or later. California residents only. Tax year 2025.

frontmatter.name: ca-540-individual-return

Covers tax year 2025 California personal income tax including the Schedule CA (540) decoupling adjustments from federal AGI, California's non-conformity with OBBBA bonus depreciation and section 174 R&E expensing, the nine-bracket rate structure (1% through 12.3% plus the 1% Mental Health Services Tax surcharge above $1M), standard and itemized deductions, California tax credits (renter's credit, CalEITC, young child tax credit), SDI/VPDI deduction, and California's own AMT. Defers estimated tax to ca-estimated-tax-540es, SMLLC franchise tax to ca-smllc-form-568, and health coverage mandate to ca-form-3853-coverage. MUST be loaded alongside us-tax-workflow-base v0.1 or later and us-federal-return-assembly. California full-year residents only.

nh-interest-dividends-note.raw:
# New Hampshire — Interest & Dividends Tax Repeal Note

_Last updated: May 22, 2026_

## Summary

New Hampshire's Interest & Dividends (I&D) Tax has been **fully repealed** effective **January 1, 2025**. No income tax return is required for tax year 2025 and beyond.

## Background

- The I&D Tax was enacted in 1923 and taxed interest and dividend income received by New Hampshire residents.
- House Bill 2, passed by the NH General Court and signed by Governor Chris Sununu during the 2023 legislative session, repealed the tax effective January 1, 2025 (originally scheduled for 2026, the repeal was accelerated).

## Phase-out schedule

| Tax year ending | Rate |
|---|---|
| Before Dec 31, 2023 | 5% |
| On or after Dec 31, 2023 | 4% |
| On or after Dec 31, 2024 | 3% |
| **On or after Jan 1, 2025** | **0% — repealed** |

## What this means for tax year 2025+

- **No I&D tax return is required** for taxable periods beginning on or after January 1, 2025.
- New Hampshire has **no statewide inc

frontmatter.name: ny-it-201-resident-return
frontmatter.description: Tier 2 New York content skill for preparing Form IT-201 (Full-Year Resident Income Tax Return) for New York State full-year residents who are sole proprietors or single-member LLCs disregarded for federal tax purposes. Covers the NYAGI computation starting from federal AGI, Form IT-225 addition and subtraction modifications (notably A-201 for unincorporated business taxes deducted federally), Form IT-558 OBBBA decoupling adjustments including the §168(k) bonus depreciation add-back and the §174A R&E expensing uncertainty, the standard vs itemized deduction decision, NY state tax computation including the $107,650 recapture worksheet, NYC resident tax computation (lines 47a-53) including the NYC UBT credit flow via Form IT-219, Yonkers resident surcharge and nonresident earnings tax (lines 55-57), MCTMT for self-employed individuals in the MCTD (lines 54a-54b), credits and payments, and the reviewer brief for the complete NY state-level return package. Does NOT cover part-year or nonresident returns (Form IT-203), itemized deduction limitations above $100,000 NYAGI in detail, PTET election scenarios, convenience-of-the-employer rule cases, NY source income allocation for multi-state activity, or NYC Unincorporated Business Tax computation itself — see Section 7. MUST be loaded alongside us-tax-workflow-base v0.2 or later. Typically loaded alongside ny-llc-filing-fee-it-204-ll (if SMLLC), nyc-unincorporated-business-tax (if NYC resident), and ny-estimated-tax-it-2105. New York State full-year residents only.

Covers the flat $25 disregarded-entity filing fee under NY Tax Law §658(c)(1), the "income, gain, loss, or deduction from New York sources" filing trigger, the dormancy exception, the March 15 due date with no extension available, late filing and late payment penalties, and the coordination with the owner's Form IT-201. Does NOT cover multi-member LLCs, LLCs taxed as partnerships or corporations, the gross-receipts-based sliding fee schedule for partnership-taxed LLCs, or LLCs doing business both inside and outside New York — see Section 7. MUST be loaded alongside us-tax-workflow-base v0.2 or later and ny-it-201-resident-return. New York full-year residents with disregarded SMLLCs only.

# Texas Franchise Tax v1.0

## What this file is

**Obligation category:** EF (Entity Fees) / IT (margin tax)
**Functional role:** Entity filing + Computation
**Status:** Complete

This is a Tier 2 content skill for computing and filing the Texas franchise tax for single-member LLCs. Texas has no personal income tax, but the franchise tax (also called the "margin tax") applies to most legal entities doing business in Texas, including SMLLCs. Sole proprietors without an LLC are NOT subject to franchise tax.

global-cloud-infrastructure.Notes: Monthly USD billing; consolidated invoice. Some accounts use AWS EMEA SARL (Luxembourg) for EU customers; AWS Inc for US.

global-cloud-infrastructure.VAT/GST notes: EU B2B: reverse charge (supplier in IE — Microsoft Ireland Operations Ltd).

global-cloud-infrastructure.VAT/GST notes: EU B2B: reverse charge (Cloudflare London Ltd for UK; Cloudflare Inc for US).

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: $5 per square foot of dedicated home office space
global-home-office.Requirement: Space must be used "regularly and exclusively" for business. Cannot be used for personal purposes.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Proportional share of actual home expenses: mortgage interest/rent, utilities, insurance, repairs, depreciation. Proportion = office sq ft / total home sq ft.
global-home-office.Requirement: Same "regularly and exclusively" test. Must complete Form 8829 (Expenses for Business Use of Your Home).
global-home-office.Ceiling: Deduction limited to gross income from the business (cannot create a loss from home office). Excess carries forward.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Based on hours worked at home per month: 25-50 hours = **GBP 10/month**; 51-100 hours = **GBP 18/month**; 101+ hours = **GBP 26/month**
global-home-office.Requirement: Must work from home. No dedicated room required. Hours must be tracked.
global-home-office.Ceiling: No ceiling beyond the rates above.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Proportional share of actual costs: rent/mortgage interest, council tax, utilities, insurance, broadband. Proportion = rooms used for business / total rooms (or floor area).
global-home-office.Requirement: Must be able to identify the portion used for business.
global-home-office.Ceiling: No statutory ceiling, but must be reasonable and supportable.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: **EUR 6 per day** worked from home
global-home-office.Requirement: No dedicated room required. Available to anyone who works from home on a given day. Cannot claim commute (Entfernungspauschale) for the same day.
global-home-office.Citation: EStG Section 4 Abs. 5 Satz 1 Nr. 6c (post-2023 reform)

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Full actual costs of the room: proportional rent, utilities, insurance, cleaning, repairs. Proportion = room area / total living area.
global-home-office.Requirement: Room must be a **separate room** used almost exclusively (>90%) for work. Must be the **center of your professional activity** (Mittelpunkt der gesamten beruflichen Tatigkeit).
global-home-office.Ceiling: **Unlimited** if it is the Mittelpunkt. If not Mittelpunkt: no deduction (only Tagespauschale available since 2023 reform).

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: **67 cents per hour** worked from home (from 1 July 2023). Covers electricity, gas, stationery, computer consumables, internet, phone.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must keep a record of actual hours worked from home (log, timesheet, roster, or diary).
global-home-office.Ceiling: No cap on hours, but must be reasonable and supported by records.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Calculate actual costs for each expense category: electricity, gas, phone, internet, stationery, computer depreciation, furniture depreciation, cleaning. Apportion each by business-use percentage.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must keep detailed records of every expense and the business-use calculation for each.
global-home-office.Citation: ATO general deduction provisions s8-1 ITAA 1997

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: **CAD 2 per day** worked from home
global-home-office.Requirement: Originally COVID-era. Extended through 2024. Check if extended to 2025+. No dedicated space required.
global-home-office.Citation: CRA — was temporary; verify current status

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Proportional share of actual expenses: rent, utilities, internet, maintenance, property tax. For self-employed: also mortgage interest, insurance, capital cost allowance. Proportion = work area / total area, multiplied by business-use time percentage.
global-home-office.Requirement: Employer (if employed) must complete T2200. Self-employed: keep records. Must have a workspace that is your principal place of business OR used exclusively and on a regular and continuous basis for meeting clients.
global-home-office.Ceiling: Self-employed: home office expenses cannot exceed business income (no loss creation). Unused portion carries forward. Employees: same income limitation.
global-home-office.Citation: CRA IT-514R (archived), CRA T4002 guide for self-employed

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Proportional share of rent, electricity, water, internet, maintenance. Proportion = dedicated room area / total home area.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must have a **dedicated room** used for work. Shared-use spaces do not qualify for full deduction.
global-home-office.Ceiling: Must be proportional and reasonable. No statutory ceiling, but IRD scrutinizes excessive claims.
global-home-office.Citation: ITA (Income Tax Act) Cap. 123; IRD guidelines

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: For micro-BNC regime: included in the 34% standard abatement (no separate deduction). For regime reel / declaration controlee: URSSAF publishes annual baremes. Common approach: a fixed percentage of rent/value proportional to room area.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must actually work from home.
global-home-office.Ceiling: Included in abatement for micro regime. For reel: must be proportional and justified.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Proportional share of actual costs: rent (or notional rent if owner), charges de copropriete, electricity, heating, internet, insurance, taxe fonciere. Proportion = room area / total area, adjusted for hours of use.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must be under regime reel / declaration controlee (not micro). Must keep all justificatifs (receipts).
global-home-office.Ceiling: Proportional and reasonable. No fixed ceiling.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Calculate proportional share of utilities (suministros) based on room area / total area. Then apply **30% of that proportion** as the deductible amount.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must declare home as partially afecto (dedicated to business) on Form 036/037.
global-home-office.Ceiling: 30% of the proportional utility costs. Very restrictive.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: No separate home office deduction. The 50% deemed-expense rate covers everything.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must be under presumptive scheme (gross receipts up to INR 75 lakh with digital receipts / INR 50 lakh otherwise).
global-home-office.Ceiling: 50% of gross receipts is deemed expenses — no itemization.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Proportional share of actual expenses: rent, electricity, internet, maintenance. Proportion = office area / total area.
global-home-office.Requirement: Must file ITR-3 and maintain books of account. Must be able to demonstrate business nexus for each expense.
global-home-office.Ceiling: No statutory ceiling, but must be proportional and reasonable. CBDT may scrutinize excessive claims.

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Proportional share of actual costs: rent, mortgage interest, utilities, OZB (property tax), insurance. Proportion = room area / total area.
global-home-office.Requirement: The workspace must be a **separate room** that is NOT part of the living space. Essentially, it must be a room you would not use if you did not work from home (e.g., a converted garage, attic room with separate entrance, or garden office).
global-home-office.Ceiling: No statutory ceiling. Must be proportional.
global-home-office.Citation: Wet IB 2001, art. 3.16 lid 1; HR 4 February 2005 (zelfstandige ruimte)

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Simplified method
global-home-office.Requirement: Simplified rate

global-home-office.Requirement: $5/sq ft

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Yes
global-home-office.Requirement: 67c/hour

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Yes
global-home-office.Requirement: CAD 2/day

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: No
global-home-office.Requirement: N/A

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: Partial (via micro abatement)
global-home-office.Requirement: 34% flat abatement (micro)

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: No
global-home-office.Requirement: N/A

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: No (44ADA covers all)
global-home-office.Requirement: 50% deemed expenses

global-home-office.Rate/Formula: No
global-home-office.Requirement: N/A

global-marketplaces-banking-fees.Fee structure: Listing fee + 6.5% transaction + 3% + USD 0.25 payment processing
global-marketplaces-banking-fees.VAT/GST notes: Etsy Ireland for EU; collects EU OSS VAT on B2C
EU marketplace deemed-supplier rules; collects VAT on imported goods ≤ EUR 150 (IOSS)

global-marketplaces-banking-fees.Bank statement variations: `AMAZON SELLER`, `AMAZON PAYMENTS`, `AMAZON SERVICES EUROPE`, `AMAZON MKTPLACE PMTS`
global-marketplaces-banking-fees.Fee structure: Referral fee 6-45% by category; FBA fees; ad fees
global-marketplaces-banking-fees.VAT/GST notes: Amazon collects "marketplace tax" in US states; EU/UK deemed-supplier for imports ≤ EUR/£150

global-marketplaces-banking-fees.Fee structure: Buyer-side fee (no seller fee); shipping fee
5% + USD 0.50 + Stripe; collects sales tax for sellers

global-marketplaces-banking-fees.Bank statement variations: `FX FEE`, `FOREIGN EXCH FEE`, `CURRENCY CONVERSION`, `0.5% INTL FEE`

global-marketplaces-banking-fees.Fee structure: Bank charges (note: deductibility may be restricted by purpose)

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Net payout after Stripe fees (typically 2.9% + 30c per transaction, varies by country/product)
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT gross revenue. NOT a Stripe subscription fee.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Book gross revenue from Stripe dashboard. Book Stripe fees as "Bank charges / Payment processing fees". The bank deposit is merely the settlement.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Payout from a Stripe Connect platform (e.g., marketplace payout)
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT revenue from YOUR customers. The platform is paying you.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Revenue = payout + platform fees withheld. Check platform dashboard for gross amounts.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Stripe Atlas incorporation service fee ($500 one-time + optional ongoing)
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: One-time: Capitalize as incorporation cost or expense as startup cost. Ongoing: Office expenses / Legal services.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Stripe's invoicing/billing product collecting payment on your behalf
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT revenue — same net-payout rule applies.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Same as standard Stripe payout rule.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Net payout from PayPal balance to bank account
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT revenue. This is a transfer between your own accounts.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: The revenue was already recorded when the customer paid into your PayPal account. This transfer is a balance movement, not income.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Payment TO a merchant via PayPal (expense)
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT revenue. NOT a PayPal fee.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Classify based on the merchant name after the asterisk. This is an expense paid via PayPal.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: A payment received via PayPal (shows on PayPal statement, not always on bank)
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: The net amount (after PayPal's fee of ~2.9% + fixed).
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Gross revenue = net received + PayPal fee. Obtain PayPal transaction report for exact fee breakdown.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: PayPal charge — could be incoming or outgoing
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: Ambiguous — must check direction and reference.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Check debit/credit. If debit: expense. If credit: net payout (not revenue).

global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT revenue. NOT an expense (unless the Wise fee itself).
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: If sending money: the transfer is a payment for an expense (classify the underlying expense). If receiving: it is a client payment arriving via Wise. The Wise fee is a separate deductible "Bank charges / FX fees" expense.
Deductible as "Bank charges / Transfer fees".

global-payment-processors.What it IS: FX conversion within Wise multi-currency account
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT revenue or expense. It is a balance conversion.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Book the FX gain/loss. The conversion itself is not income or expense — only the exchange rate difference matters.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Net payout from Square card reader
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Same pattern as Stripe. Gross revenue = payout + Square processing fee (typically 2.6% + 10c US). Obtain Square dashboard report.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Purchase of Square card reader hardware
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Capitalize as equipment (if material) or expense as office supplies.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Net payout from SumUp card reader
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Same pattern. SumUp fee varies by country (1.69% EU typical). Check SumUp dashboard.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Net payout from Zettle (now PayPal Zettle) card reader
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Same pattern. Now owned by PayPal. Fee typically 1.75% EU.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Transfer between Revolut and external bank, or Revolut account fee
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT revenue. NOT an expense (unless it is the account fee).
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: If a transfer to/from your own account: ignore for P&L (balance movement). If a fee: "Bank charges". If a payment to a supplier: classify by supplier.
"Bank charges / Account fees". Verify business vs. personal plan.

global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT gross revenue. GoCardless deducts its fee before payout.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Gross revenue = payout + GoCardless fees (1% + 20p typical UK). Obtain GoCardless report.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: A direct debit payment collected FROM you by a vendor using GoCardless
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: This is an EXPENSE, not revenue.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Classify based on the vendor name. Common for recurring SaaS bills collected via DD.

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Payout from Paddle as Merchant of Record (MoR)
global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT your gross revenue. Paddle invoiced the customer, not you. YOUR revenue is the net payout from Paddle.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Paddle is the legal seller. They handle VAT, refunds, chargebacks. Your revenue = Paddle payout. Paddle's cut includes their fee + taxes they remitted. Do NOT also record the customer's gross payment as your revenue.

global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT your gross revenue. Same MoR pattern as Paddle.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Revenue = Gumroad payout. Gumroad handles sales tax/VAT and takes their commission.

global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT your gross revenue. Same MoR pattern.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Revenue = LemonSqueezy payout. They handle all tax obligations as MoR.
Same as Stripe pattern. Shopify Payments fee is separate. Note: Shopify subscription fee is a different charge (SaaS expense).
Revenue = payout + Amazon Pay fees.

global-payment-processors.What it is NOT: NOT gross revenue. Klarna pays you in full (minus fee) even though customer pays in installments.
global-payment-processors.Classification rule: Revenue = Klarna payout + Klarna merchant fee. You recognize full revenue at sale, not when customer completes installments.
Same net-payout pattern. Adyen fees deducted.
Same pattern. Common in NL/EU.
Description
Net
Paddle fee (5%)

global-payment-processors.What it IS: Already deducted from your payout — do NOT double-deduct
MoR reports and remits — you do NOT include in your VAT return
Issued to the MoR (or not needed — check your contract)

global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: EU: VAT exempt (international) or zero-rated. Domestic flights: local VAT may apply.
International flights: zero-rated/exempt. UK domestic: standard rate.
DE entity. International: exempt. DE domestic: 19% VAT.
FR entity. International: exempt. FR domestic: 10% TVA.
US entity. US domestic: 7.5% excise tax (not reclaimable).
AU entity. Domestic: 10% GST (claimable). International: GST-free.
AE entity. No VAT on international flights.
ES entity. International: exempt. ES domestic: 10% IVA.
NL entity. Acts as agent — VAT on service fee only. Flight itself follows airline VAT rules.
US entity. Agent model — VAT varies.

global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Skyscanner redirects to airlines/agents. The charge is from the actual seller.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: N/A — Skyscanner does not charge directly.
global-travel-expenses.Notes: If you see a Skyscanner charge, it is likely through a partner.

global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: CZ entity. Acts as agent or MoR depending on booking.

global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Google redirects — charge is from airline/agent.
global-travel-expenses.Notes: Google Flights does not sell tickets directly.

global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: NL entity (Booking.com B.V.). Hotel charges VAT, not Booking.com. Booking.com's commission is a separate B2B service.
global-travel-expenses.Notes: Booking.com is an agent — the hotel is the supplier.

global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: IE entity for EU (Airbnb Ireland UC). Airbnb is a MARKETPLACE — VAT treatment differs from hotels. Airbnb charges VAT on its service fee. The host may or may not charge VAT.
global-travel-expenses.Notes: Airbnb invoices are split: accommodation (from host) + service fee (from Airbnb). Only Airbnb's service fee has clear VAT treatment.

global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: US entity (Expedia group). Agent model in most markets.
Check if merchant model or agency model — VAT treatment differs.
Hotel charges local VAT/tourism tax at the rate of the hotel's country.
FR entity. Local VAT applies at hotel location.
NL entity for EU (Uber B.V.). UK: Uber charges 20% VAT. US: no sales tax on most rides.
global-travel-expenses.Notes: Uber provides VAT invoices in most EU/UK markets.

global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: EE entity. EU: local VAT rules apply.
UK/FR/BE entity depending on route. Zero-rated for international segments in UK.
FR entity. 10% TVA on domestic rail.
DE entity. 7% VAT on domestic passenger transport.
IT entity. 10% IVA on domestic rail.
ES entity. 10% IVA on domestic rail.
NL entity. 9% BTW on domestic rail.
UK entity. Zero-rated public transport. No VAT to reclaim.

global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Partially deductible (varies by country — see table below)
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: Local VAT on food/drink at local rates.
global-travel-expenses.Notes: Must be during business travel. Meals at home city are generally NOT deductible (unless client entertainment).

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: 50% of actual cost (meals while travelling for business)
global-travel-expenses.Category: Must be away from "tax home". Lavish/extravagant meals disallowed. No deduction for meals with no business purpose.
global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: The 100% temporary deduction for restaurant meals expired after 2022. Back to 50% for 2023+.

global-travel-expenses.Category: Must be "wholly and exclusively" for business. Subsistence while travelling on business qualifies. Regular commute meals do NOT qualify.
global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: HMRC allows reasonable meal costs while travelling for business.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: Actual cost NOT deductible for self-employed meals. Use per-diem (Verpflegungspauschale) instead.
global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Self-employed: actual meal costs are not deductible. Only the flat per-diem rate applies.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: Actual cost minus "normal meal cost" (repas pris a domicile). 2025: approx. EUR 5.35 deducted per meal.
global-travel-expenses.Category: Only the excess over normal home-eating cost is deductible. Cap of ~EUR 20.70 per meal.
Must be travelling overnight for business. Day-trip meals generally not deductible.
Must be while travelling for business (away from metropolitan area for 12+ hours).

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: Deductible up to EUR 26.67/day (domestic) or EUR 48.08/day (international)
global-travel-expenses.Category: These are the maximum amounts. Excess is not deductible.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: 75% of actual cost (up to 2% of revenue)

global-travel-expenses.Category: Must be for business travel. Personal meals not deductible.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: Varies by city (GSA rates). Range: $59-$79/day for meals & incidentals (M&IE). High-cost cities up to $79.
global-travel-expenses.Category: Set by State Department per destination city.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: GSA rates updated annually at gsa.gov. Can use "high-low" simplified method: $310/$75 (high-cost) or $220/$75 (other).

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: Benchmark scale rates: Breakfast GBP 11, Lunch GBP 11, Dinner GBP 26, plus incidentals GBP 5-10/night.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: HMRC Employment Income Manual EIM05231. Sole traders can use these as a benchmark.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: EUR 16/day (travel 8-24 hours), EUR 32/day (24-hour absence), EUR 16 for arrival/departure days.
global-travel-expenses.Category: Varies by country (BMF publishes list annually). E.g., US $66, UK GBP 52, FR EUR 58 for 24-hour absence.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: Verpflegungspauschale. First 3 months of same location only. After 3 months at same destination: no more per-diem.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: Forfait repas: ~EUR 20.70/meal (employee). Self-employed (BNC): actual costs or forfait at URSSAF rates.
global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Must be away from normal workplace.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: ATO reasonable travel allowance rates (updated annually). 2024-25: varies by salary band. Tier 1 (lowest salary): AUD 131.65/day (meals), AUD 309/night (accommodation capital cities).
Treasury Board rates. 2025: CAD 23.40 breakfast, CAD 23.55 lunch, CAD 52.15 dinner, CAD 17.30 incidentals = CAD 116.40/day.
global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Must be away from metropolitan area 12+ hours.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: Only 50% of meal per-diem is deductible for tax (same as actual).

global-travel-expenses.Category: See domestic column — international rate is higher.
global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Must be outside municipality of workplace.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: Reglamento IRPF art. 9. These are tax-free maximums for employees. Self-employed: consult advisor.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: EUR 46.48/day (domestic lump sum) or actual with 75% deductibility.
global-travel-expenses.Deductible?: Must be outside municipality of workplace.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: Complex interaction between 75% meal cap and 2%-of-revenue overall cap.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: No official per-diem for self-employed. Must use actual costs (80% deductible for meals).
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: NL does not offer per-diem for zelfstandigen.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: No statutory per-diem for self-employed. Actual costs only.
No official per-diem. Actual costs, must demonstrate business purpose.
global-travel-expenses.VAT treatment: IRD practice: reasonable costs accepted with documentation.
Local entity. Local VAT on office rental. Most jurisdictions: standard-rated.

global-travel-expenses.Notes: Many small co-working spaces appear under their own name.

global-travel-expenses.Vendor: NOT deductible (primary purpose is personal) in US. Apportioned in some other jurisdictions.
100% transport if primary purpose is conference
Only the employee/freelancer's costs. No deduction for companion.
global-travel-expenses.Category: Single-room rate only (not the couple supplement).

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: **70 cents per mile** (2025, IRS announced rate — verify annually)
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Must maintain a contemporaneous mileage log: date, destination, business purpose, miles driven.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: No personal miles can be claimed. Must have records to substantiate business vs. personal split.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Total actual vehicle costs (gas, oil, tires, repairs, insurance, registration, depreciation/lease payments) multiplied by (business miles / total miles).
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Same log requirement. Also need all receipts for actual expenses.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Depreciation limits (luxury auto caps): Section 280F. Year 1: $12,400 (2025, verify). Bonus depreciation may increase first-year limit.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: First 10,000 business miles: **45p per mile**. Above 10,000 miles: **25p per mile**.
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Must maintain a mileage log with date, destination, purpose, and miles.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Only business miles claimed. Personal miles excluded.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Total actual costs (fuel, insurance, servicing, repairs, road tax, MOT, depreciation/capital allowances) multiplied by business-use percentage.
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Need a mileage log to determine business-use percentage AND receipts for all costs.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Must apportion between business and personal.
global-vehicle-expenses.Citation: ITTOIA 2005 s34; CAA 2001 for capital allowances

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: **30 cents per km** for one-way distance (first 20 km). **38 cents per km** from km 21 onward. Only for commute to primary workplace.
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: No log required for commute — only the distance.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Only counts actual working days. Maximum one trip per working day.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Actual total vehicle costs (fuel, insurance, tax, depreciation, repairs, garage) multiplied by (business km / total km).
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. **Extremely strict logbook** (Fahrtenbuch): each trip must record date, departure/arrival location, route, purpose, km reading at start and end. Must be bound or electronic (BMF-approved format).
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Only the business-trip proportion. Commute is separately handled by Entfernungspauschale.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: If using the car for both business and personal: add 1% of the car's gross list price per month as taxable benefit. Plus 0.03% x list price x km for commute.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Simplification: you avoid the logbook but accept a deemed private-use addition.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: **88 cents per km** (2024-25 income year — verify annually)
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: No detailed log required. Must be able to show how you calculated business km (reasonable estimate accepted).
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Maximum **5,000 business km** per vehicle per year = **AUD 4,400 max deduction**.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Actual total vehicle costs multiplied by business-use percentage (determined by a 12-week representative logbook sample).
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Must keep a **12-week logbook** (continuous period). The logbook is valid for 5 years unless circumstances change materially. Must keep all receipts.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: No km cap. Business-use percentage from the logbook applies to total costs including depreciation.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: First 5,000 business km: **70 cents per km**. Each additional km: **64 cents per km**. (2025 rates — verify annually. Yukon/NWT/Nunavut: add 4c/km.)
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Must maintain a logbook: date, destination, purpose, km driven.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Only business km. Must separate business and personal use with a log.
global-vehicle-expenses.Citation: CRA Motor Vehicle Expenses guide (T4002); Income Tax Regulations s7306

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Total actual costs (fuel, insurance, maintenance, licence, interest on car loan, CCA/depreciation) multiplied by (business km / total km).
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Same logbook. Also keep all receipts.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: CCA (capital cost allowance) limits: Class 10 vehicle max cost is **CAD 37,000** (2025 — verify). Passenger vehicle interest deduction capped at **CAD 300/month**. Lease costs capped at **CAD 1,050/month**.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Vehicle costs are generally **NOT deductible** for autonomos unless you are in a profession where a vehicle is essential (commercial agents, transport, delivery, taxi, sales representatives).
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: N/A for most. If eligible: yes, logbook needed.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: For most autonomos: **0% deductible** for IRPF.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: **50% of IVA** on vehicle purchase and expenses is deductible (presumed 50% business use). Can claim 100% if you prove 100% business use (very difficult).
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: 50% IVA deduction cap unless full business use proven.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Vehicle expenses are deductible at **20% of actual costs** for self-employed professionals. Fuel, insurance, repairs, depreciation — all at 20%.
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Keep all receipts and invoices. No formal logbook, but recommended.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Depreciation base capped at **EUR 18,075.99** for cars. Fuel deductible at 20%.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: **80% of actual costs** deductible.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Depreciation base capped at **EUR 25,822.84**.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Total actual costs (fuel, insurance, road tax, depreciation, maintenance, financing costs) minus private-use adjustment.
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Yes. Must maintain a logbook (rittenadministratie) if claiming more than incidental business use.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: If private use > 500 km/year: must add back a deemed private-use amount (bijtelling) — 22% of catalogue value per year for most cars, 16% for zero-emission.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: The Netherlands does NOT offer a per-km deduction rate for self-employed.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: N/A.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Actual fuel, maintenance, insurance, depreciation costs attributable to business use.
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Must keep receipts and demonstrate business nexus. No formal logbook format prescribed.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Must show business nexus. Presumptive scheme (44ADA) does not allow separate vehicle claims.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Cars: 15% WDV (written-down value) per year. Commercial vehicles: 30% WDV.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Depreciation applies only to the business-use portion.
global-vehicle-expenses.Citation: IT Act 1961, Section 32; CBDT depreciation table

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Actual fuel, insurance, licence fees, repairs, depreciation — proportioned by business-use percentage.
global-vehicle-expenses.Log required?: Must keep fuel receipts and a business-use log or estimate.
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Must be proportional. No formal cap, but IRD reviews reasonableness.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Wear and tear allowance: typically 20% per year on cost (5-year write-off for motor vehicles).
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Applied to total cost, then proportioned by business-use percentage.

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Per-km/mile rate available?
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Cap on simplified

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Yes
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: No mile cap (but actual expenses may be better)

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Yes
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: No cap

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Only for commute (30c/38c per km)
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: Commute only; business trips = actual

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Yes
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: 5,000 km/year

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: Yes
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: No km cap

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: No
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: N/A

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: No
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: N/A

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: No (EUR 0.23/km for employees only)
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: N/A

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: No
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: N/A

global-vehicle-expenses.Rate/Formula: No
global-vehicle-expenses.Business use cap: N/A

frontmatter.name: dac6-mdr-reportable-arrangements
frontmatter.Use this skill whenever an intermediary: domestic-only tax shelter disclosure regimes (e.g., US §6111 reportable transactions, UK DOTAS, Canada §237.3), country-by-country reporting (BEPS Action 13), or FATCA/CRS automatic exchange (see fatca-crs-automatic-exchange). ALWAYS read this skill before advising on whether a cross-border arrangement triggers reporting.

table.Trigger: The person designs, markets, organises, makes available, or manages implementation

The person provides aid, assistance, or advice with respect to the design/marketing/organising/availability/implementation, *and* a reasonable person in the same position would have known the arrangement is reportable
Arrangement where the relevant taxpayer / participant undertakes to comply with a confidentiality condition that may require them not to disclose how the arrangement could secure a tax advantage.
The intermediary is entitled to a fee fixed by reference to (a) the tax advantage derived OR (b) whether a tax advantage is actually derived (e.g., contingency fee).
The arrangement has substantially standardised documentation or structure, available to more than one taxpayer, without need for substantial customisation.
A participant takes contrived steps consisting in acquiring a loss-making company, discontinuing its main activity, and using the losses in order to reduce its tax liability.
Conversion of income into capital, gifts, or other categories of revenue taxed at a lower level or exempt.
Circular transactions resulting in the round-tripping of funds through entities without primary commercial function or transactions that offset / cancel each other.

An arrangement that may have the effect of undermining the reporting obligation under the laws implementing the EU automatic exchange of information legislation (CRS / DAC2) or that takes advantage of the absence of such legislation. Covers eight specific sub-features (D.1(a)–(g)) reflecting the OECD Model MDR on CRS Avoidance Arrangements.

An arrangement involving a non-transparent legal or beneficial ownership chain with the use of persons, legal arrangements or structures (a) that do not carry on substantive economic activity supported by adequate staff, equipment, assets and premises; (b) that are incorporated, managed, resident, controlled or established in any jurisdiction other than the jurisdiction of residence of one or more of the beneficial owners of the assets held; and (c) where the beneficial owners are made unidentifiable.
An arrangement that involves the use of unilateral safe harbour rules.

frontmatter.name: non-eu-export-services
frontmatter.description: Use this skill whenever a freelancer or small business sells services to a client located outside their own VAT/GST jurisdiction and needs to determine the correct indirect tax treatment. This skill covers the rules for EU sellers exporting services outside the EU, UK sellers to non-UK clients, Australian sellers (GST-free exports under Division 38), Indian sellers (zero-rated export of services), and Singaporean sellers (zero-rated under the Fifth Schedule). frontmatter.name: permanent-establishment-risk
frontmatter.description: Use this skill whenever a freelancer or small business has cross-border activity that might create a tax presence (Permanent Establishment / PE) in the client's country. Trigger on phrases like "permanent establishment", "PE risk", "tax presence", "183-day rule", "fixed place of business", "dependent agent", "service PE", "effectively connected income", "do I need to file taxes in my client's country", "working abroad for client", "remote work PE", or any request about whether a freelancer's cross-border activity triggers a filing obligation in another country. This skill contains the OECD model treaty PE definition, country-specific PE thresholds, the 183-day rule, remote work analysis, avoidance strategies, and consequences of PE creation. This is primarily T2/T3 material -- flag for professional review in all but the most straightforward cases. ALWAYS read this skill before advising on any PE-related question.

Tier 3: multi-country PE assessment, profit attribution, anti-avoidance rules, actual PE triggered -- full professional review required.
A physical location: office, room, desk, workstation, construction site

table.Value: US taxes foreign persons on "effectively connected income" (ECI) -- income connected with a US trade or business. Even a single day of business activity in the US COULD create ECI.
Most US treaties include an Art 5 PE definition. Without a PE, business profits are exempt.

OECD-standard 183-day rule for employees; no specific service PE for independent contractors under most US treaties. BUT: US domestic law is aggressive -- if no treaty exists, any US-source business income may be taxed.
If PE exists: file Form 1120-F (corporations) or Form 1040-NR (individuals).
A freelancer who works entirely remotely from outside the US for a US client has NO PE risk. A freelancer who works on-site at a US client's office for extended periods may create a PE.
India taxes non-residents on income that "accrues or arises in India." Services performed in India trigger Indian tax.
Many Indian treaties include a **90-day service PE** (shorter than the standard 183 days). India-US treaty: service PE at 90 days in a fiscal year. India-UK treaty: 90 days.
90 days in many treaties (check specific treaty).
A freelancer who visits India for a project exceeding 90 days in a year will likely trigger a service PE. Remote work from outside India: no PE.
India also imposes WHT on service fees (see `withholding-tax-matrix.md`), which is SEPARATE from PE. WHT applies even without PE.

UAE has treaties with many countries. PE defined per OECD model.
**Minimal.** Even if PE exists, the tax rate is 0% (below AED 375,000) or 9%. For a freelancer earning below the threshold, PE is irrelevant. No personal income tax in UAE.

Standard OECD Art 5 PE. No service PE in most German treaties.
6 months for construction PE. No specific day count for services in OECD-model treaties.

Non-residents taxed on Australian-source income. A PE creates a tax presence.

Low for remote. Medium if on-site 3+ months.

Standard OECD Art 5. Some treaties include service PE.
Low. Singapore is generally business-friendly and does not aggressively pursue PE claims against short-term visitors.
No physical presence = no PE. This is the strongest protection.
Keep visits short (under 2 weeks) and infrequent. Avoid patterns of regular presence.

frontmatter.name: vat-place-of-supply-master

table.Value: Determining the correct country for VAT on cross-border supplies of goods and services
Taxable person acting as such (business with VAT number)
Where the **customer** is established (or has a fixed establishment receiving the service)
Supplier invoices without VAT; customer self-assesses under reverse charge (Art 196 within EU)
Customer's VAT number via VIES before zero-rating
Where the **supplier** is established (or has a fixed establishment from which the service is supplied)
Supplier charges their own country's VAT rate
Numerous special rules override Art 45 for specific service types (see Section 2)
Valuation, estate agency, legal services related to property, construction, accommodation, property management, architect services
Where the **property is located** — regardless of B2B/B2C and regardless of where supplier or customer is
A German architect designing a building in Portugal must charge Portuguese VAT (or register there), even if the client is in Ireland
Freelancers forget that remote design work for foreign property still follows this rule
Where the transport takes place, **proportional to distances covered** in each country
A bus journey from Belgium through Luxembourg to Germany: VAT is split proportionally across all three countries
Where the transport takes place, proportional to distances
Place where transport begins, unless transport is between two member states — then destination

table.Field: B2B — services other than admission (e.g., organising, production)

table.Value: Where the services are **physically carried out**
See Art 57 — place of supply = point of departure within EU
Where the means of transport is **put at the customer's disposal**
Supplier's country (Art 45), except pleasure boats = where put at disposal if supplier established there
SaaS, downloads, streaming, e-books, online courses (pre-recorded), cloud hosting, telecoms, broadcasting — B2C only
Art 44 applies instead (customer's country, reverse charge)
EU-established sellers below €10,000 total cross-border B2C digital sales may charge home-country VAT
No threshold benefit; must always charge destination-country rate
Use OSS (One-Stop Shop) to report across all EU countries from a single registration
Where the goods are **located at the time of supply**
Goods sold in a German shop to a walk-in customer: German VAT
Where the goods are located when **dispatch or transport to the customer begins**
German warehouse ships goods to a German customer: place of supply = Germany
Distance selling rules (Art 33) and installation/assembly (Art 36) override this
Goods sold to consumers (B2C) or non-VAT-registered entities in another EU country
EU sellers below €10,000 total cross-border B2C goods + digital services may treat place of supply as origin country
Use Union OSS to report all intra-EU distance sales from one registration
Individual country thresholds (€35,000/€100,000) replaced by single €10,000 EU-wide threshold
The EU member state where goods **enter the EU** (Art 60)
If goods are placed under customs warehousing or transit, importation occurs where goods **leave** that arrangement (Art 61)
At importation, by the importer — collected by customs
For goods ≤€150 sold B2C, use Import One-Stop Shop to collect VAT at point of sale
Where the goods are **installed or assembled**
German manufacturer ships and installs machinery in a French factory: place of supply = France
Goods supplied A → B → C, shipped directly from A to C across EU borders
A supplier in the chain (other than the first) who dispatches or arranges transport
If B communicates to A the VAT number issued by the dispatch member state, transport is attributed to B's supply to C
Only one supply in the chain is the intra-EU zero-rated supply; the other is a domestic supply
"Nexus" determines which state can tax you
Office, warehouse, employee, or inventory in the state
Most states: $100,000 in sales OR 200 transactions/year
Taxability varies by state — SaaS taxable in ~25 states, not taxable in CA, GA, MO
Seller must collect and remit; no mechanism for buyer self-assessment in most states
No single national registration; must track nexus in 45+ states individually (or use automation like Stripe Tax, TaxJar, Avalara)
AUD 75,000/year in supplies to Australian consumers
Non-resident must register for GST and charge 10%
GST applies at point of sale since July 2018
Non-resident suppliers of OIDAR services to non-taxable Indian recipients (B2C)
Services delivered over the internet — substantially automated, minimal human intervention
Simplified registration under GST for non-resident OIDAR suppliers
Indian business self-assesses under reverse charge
Separate 2% levy on non-resident e-commerce operators (no global revenue threshold)
10% (8% reduced rate for food/beverages, not applicable to digital)
Must register and charge JCT regardless of revenue
Reverse charge applies for "specified services" received from abroad
No de minimis for non-resident digital service providers to consumers
Qualified Invoice System (QIS) since October 2023 — registered invoices required for input tax credit

table.Field: Is your client a **business** (with a VAT number)?
Is the service related to **immovable property** (Art 47)?
Is the service **admission to an event** (Art 53)?
Is it **short-term hire of transport** ≤30 days (Art 56)?
table.Value: VAT where vehicle put at disposal
→ **Art 44 applies.** Place of supply = client's country. Invoice without VAT. Client self-assesses (reverse charge in EU; self-assessment rules outside EU).

table.Field: Is it an **electronically supplied service, telecoms, or broadcasting** (Art 58)?

table.Field: Is the service related to **immovable property** (Art 47)?

table.Field: Are you shipping goods to a **consumer in another EU country**?

frontmatter.name: wealth-tax-matrix

table.Detail: Exempt if ≥5% direct ownership (or 20% group), held actively, directing functions
Items of historical / artistic value classified
No general wealth tax. IVIE (0.76% on foreign real estate) and IVAFE (0.2% on foreign financial assets) remain asset-specific. Long-term ISA proposals for super-rich resurface periodically.
Wealth tax (Vermögensteuer) suspended since 1997 (BVerfG ruling). Periodic SPD/Greens proposals; not in force as of 2025.
No annual wealth tax. Wealth Tax Commission 2020 recommended a one-off 5% wealth tax above £500k; no enactment. 2024 Labour government rejected an annual wealth tax.

No federal annual wealth tax. State-level proposals (California AB 259, New York AB 6010); proposed federal Warren/Sanders bills (§2901-2902 ITA) have not advanced. Senator Wyden's billionaire tax (proposed mark-to-market on unrealised gains) is conceptually distinct but related.

No general wealth tax. CSLL on net profits applies to companies. Proposed Imposto sobre Grandes Fortunas (CF Art. 153 §VII) authorised by Constitution but never enacted. PEC 162/2022 proposal pending.
No general wealth tax. **Securities Account Tax (Taxe sur les comptes-titres / TCT)** at 0.15% above EUR 1m per holder per account introduced 2021.
No general wealth tax. Property tax (építményadó) and similar local taxes only.

frontmatter.name: withholding-tax-matrix
frontmatter.description: Use this skill whenever a freelancer or small business receives or makes a cross-border payment and the question is whether withholding tax (WHT) applies. Trigger on phrases like "withholding tax", "WHT", "tax withheld", "double tax treaty", "treaty rate", "certificate of residence", "tax residency certificate", "form W-8BEN", "royalty withholding", "interest withholding", "TDS on services", or any request involving tax deducted at source on cross-border payments for services, royalties, or interest. This skill contains the withholding tax matrix for the top 30 country pairs, treaty rate lookups, certificate of residence requirements, and zero-WHT corridors. ALWAYS read this skill before advising on any cross-border withholding tax question.
